service.rs has some tests (authenticate response format, handle_register_agent, handle_listener_add) but significant gaps remain:

- authenticate() wrong-password and malformed-message rejection (was red-cell-c2-n9tys)
- handle_agent_message sub-handlers: BODY_AGENT_TASK, BODY_AGENT_REGISTER, BODY_AGENT_RESPONSE (was red-cell-c2-jnb9q)
- handle_agent_output dedicated test (was red-cell-c2-d71tn)
- WebSocket integration test for auth + dispatch flow (was red-cell-c2-w59il)

title: test: plugin deadlock scenario lacks reproduction test
description: teamserver/src/plugins.rs — No test exercises the scenario where a Python callback invokes a Rust API that tries to acquire the global plugin runtime Mutex. A regression test should set up a plugin that calls back into the teamserver API from within a callback, verifying it either succeeds or returns a clear error (not a deadlock).
close_reason: Added regression test callback_reentrant_rust_api_does_not_deadlock that exercises a Python callback calling havoc.Agent().info during callback dispatch, verifying the CALLBACK_RUNTIME thread-local bypass prevents deadlock (with 10s timeout guard).

title: test: mock_demon_agent_checkin lacks test for multiple concurrent agents on same listener
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/mock_demon_agent_checkin.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

All existing tests register a single agent per test. There is no test verifying that two agents with different IDs and different AES keys can simultaneously register on the same HTTP listener and independently receive their own tasks. A bug in agent-key lookup or task-queue isolation would only surface with concurrent agents.

**Suggested test approach**:
Register agent A (key_a) and agent B (key_b) on the same listener. Queue a task for agent A. Have agent B poll `CommandGetJob` — it must receive no jobs. Have agent A poll — it must receive exactly its task. Verify both callbacks decrypt correctly with their respective keys.
close_reason: consolidated into red-cell-c2-nub5t

title: test: SOCKS5 relay missing test for unsupported command types (BIND, UDP ASSOCIATE)
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/socks5_relay.rs
**Gap type**: error path

The SOCKS5 tests cover CONNECT (0x01) handshakes for IPv4, domain, and IPv6 address types, but there is no test for the other two SOCKS5 commands: BIND (0x02) and UDP ASSOCIATE (0x03). The relay should reject these with an appropriate SOCKS5 error reply (e.g., command not supported, REP=0x07) rather than hanging, panicking, or silently accepting.

**Suggested test approach**:
After completing the SOCKS5 no-auth handshake, send a CONNECT request with command byte 0x02 (BIND). Read the reply and assert the REP field indicates command-not-supported. Verify no job is enqueued in the agent registry.
close_reason: Added integration tests for SOCKS5 BIND (0x02) and UDP ASSOCIATE (0x03) command rejection, verifying REP=0x07 reply and no agent job enqueued

title: test: dispatch/util.rs basename and arch-label functions have no tests
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/util.rs
**Gap type**: existence

Three pure utility functions in this file have zero tests:

1. `basename(path: &str) -> String` — strips the directory prefix from Windows or POSIX paths. Edge cases not tested: empty string input (returns the original), path consisting only of separators (e.g. "/", "\\"), path with no separator (already a basename), mixed separator types, and trailing separator.

2. `process_arch_label(value: u32) -> &'static str` — maps the Demon protocol arch field (1=x86, 2=x64, 3=IA64, other=Unknown). The Unknown/fallthrough branch is untested.

3. `windows_arch_label(value: u32) -> &'static str` — maps WinAPI PROCESSOR_ARCHITECTURE values (0=x86, 9=x64/AMD64, 5=ARM, 12=ARM64, 6=Itanium). Five known values and the Unknown fallback are all untested.

A wrong mapping in `process_arch_label` or `windows_arch_label` would display the wrong architecture label in the operator console without any test catching it.

**Suggested test approach**:
Add a short `#[cfg(test)]` block at the bottom of the file. For `basename` test the empty string, no-separator, POSIX, Windows, and mixed cases. For the arch labels, loop over all known numeric values and the unknown value and assert the expected string.
close_reason: Added tests for process_arch_label (x86/x64/IA64/Unknown) and windows_arch_label (x86/x64/AMD64/ARM/ARM64/Itanium/Unknown) in dispatch::util.rs. All basename tests were already present.

title: Implement session table (agent list panel)
description: In crates/client: egui table panel showing all active agents. Columns: ID, hostname, username, domain, IP, PID, process, arch, elevated, OS, sleep/jitter, last checkin. Sortable columns, color coding (elevated = red highlight). Right-click context menu: interact, kill, add note. Double-click to open command console. Filter/search bar.
close_reason: Implemented sortable session table with search, console launch, and agent actions

title: test: AgentLivenessConfig::from_profile with zero Demon Sleep uses minimum timeout
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/agent_liveness.rs
**Gap type**: edge case / boundary condition

`AgentLivenessConfig::from_profile` filters out zero sleep with `.filter(|sleep| *sleep > 0)`, making `default_sleep_secs = None`. The effective timeout then falls back to `MIN_SWEEP_INTERVAL_SECS.saturating_mul(3)` = 3 seconds. A sweep interval of 1 second results.

No test covers `Sleep = 0` in the profile. A regression here (e.g., treating zero sleep as a valid agent timeout) would cause every agent to be marked dead within 3 seconds of the monitor starting — a production-breaking bug that would be invisible until deployment.

**Suggested test approach**:
Build a profile with `Sleep = 0` and no `AgentTimeoutSecs`, call `AgentLivenessConfig::from_profile`, assert `sweep_interval == Duration::from_secs(1)` and that `timeout_for` with a sample agent (sleep_delay=0) returns 3.
close_reason: Added exact sweep_interval==1s assertion and timeout_for(sleep_delay=0)==3 assertion to the zero-sleep test

title: test: is_legacy_sha3_digest boundary detection has no direct unit tests
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/auth.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

The `is_legacy_sha3_digest` function (line 582) is the security gate that determines whether a stored password verifier is a raw SHA3-256 hex digest (legacy format) vs an Argon2 PHC string. It checks for exactly 64 hex characters. This function has no direct unit tests — it is only exercised indirectly through the `from_profile_with_database_upgrades_legacy_runtime_operator_digests` integration test.

Missing edge cases: 63-char hex string (should return false), 65-char hex string (should return false), 64-char string with a non-hex char at position 32 (should return false), valid Argon2 PHC string (should return false). A bug here could cause the auth service to misinterpret Argon2 hashes as legacy digests or vice versa, breaking authentication.

**Suggested test approach**:
Add direct unit tests: 64-char all-hex → true, 63-char hex → false, 65-char hex → false, 64-char with non-hex → false, Argon2 PHC string → false, empty string → false.
close_reason: Added 6 is_legacy_sha3_digest boundary tests: valid 64-char hex, 63/65 char, non-hex char, argon2 string, empty

title: test: listener_lifecycle does not test duplicate listener name rejection
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/listener_lifecycle.rs
**Gap type**: error path

No test verifies what happens when `manager.create()` is called with a listener name that already exists. This is a basic input validation scenario — if the server silently overwrites the existing listener config, it could cause data loss or orphaned runtime tasks.

**Suggested test approach**:
Create a listener with name `lc-dup`, then call `create()` again with the same name but a different port. Assert the second call returns an error. Verify the original listener config is unchanged via `manager.summary()`.
close_reason: Test already exists and passes

title: Implement loot viewer and chat panel
description: In crates/client: loot viewer panel (credentials, files, screenshots - filterable by type/agent/time, download button for files). Multi-operator chat panel (message input + scrollable history with operator names and timestamps, broadcasts via WebSocket chat messages).
close_reason: Implemented filterable loot viewer, chat input/history, and file download handling in the client

title: test: rbac session_token rejects non-Bearer authorization schemes
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/rbac.rs
**Gap type**: error path

The `session_token` helper (line 271) correctly rejects non-Bearer authorization headers via `strip_prefix("Bearer ")`, but no test verifies this path. The existing tests cover empty bearer tokens, non-UTF8 headers, missing tokens, and x-session-token fallback — but never the case where the Authorization header contains a different scheme (e.g., `Basic xyz` or `Token abc`). This is security-relevant because a misconfigured proxy or client could send the wrong scheme.

**Suggested test approach**:
Send a request with `Authorization: Basic dXNlcjpwYXNz` and assert the extractor returns `AuthorizationError::InvalidAuthorizationHeader`.
close_reason: Test already exists: authenticated_operator_extractor_rejects_non_bearer_authorization_scheme — passes successfully

title: Teamserver - Python Plugin System
description: Embed Python (via PyO3) in the teamserver to support operator-side scripting modules. Expose API bindings: RegisterCommand, RegisterCallback, agent/listener access. Load .py modules from a configured directory. Preserves compatibility with existing Havoc Python modules where possible.
close_reason: Implemented the embedded teamserver Python runtime with plugin loading, agent/listener bindings, callback dispatch, and Havoc-compatible command registration.

title: Unit tests for common crate
description: Comprehensive unit tests for crates/common. Protocol: round-trip encoding/decoding of all Demon packet types, edge cases (empty data, max size). Crypto: AES-256-CBC encrypt/decrypt, known-answer tests against Havoc-produced ciphertext. JSON WebSocket messages: serialize/deserialize all event types. Config parsing: test against all three existing .yaotl profile files. Target 90%+ coverage.
close_reason: Added comprehensive common crate protocol, crypto, operator message, and profile fixture tests.

title: test: app.rs build_router with service_bridge=Some never exercised
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/app.rs
**Gap type**: existence

`build_router` (line 128) conditionally merges `service_routes(bridge)` when `state.service_bridge` is `Some`. Every inline test in `app.rs` constructs the test state with `service_bridge: None`, so the `router.merge(service_routes(bridge))` branch is never executed in unit tests.

A broken `service_routes` function signature or a panicking route registration would not be caught until integration testing. While separate service bridge issues exist for deeper testing, the basic branch of 'router builds successfully with service_bridge set' deserves a smoke test.

**Suggested test approach**:
Construct a `TeamserverState` with a real `ServiceBridge` instance and call `build_router(state)`. Assert that the resulting router responds to a known service route path (even if with an auth error) rather than 404.
close_reason: Added smoke test in app.rs that constructs TeamserverState with service_bridge=Some(ServiceBridge) and verifies the service endpoint is routable (not 404) after build_router merges service_routes.

title: test: HTTP listener pipeline lacks concurrent multi-agent init stress test
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/http_listener_pipeline.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

The HTTP listener pipeline tests cover single-agent init, duplicate init (same agent_id), and malformed bodies thoroughly. However, there is no test for **multiple distinct agents performing DEMON_INIT concurrently** against the same listener.

In production, a listener may receive init packets from multiple newly-deployed agents within the same second. Race conditions in the agent registry insert path, the event bus broadcast, or the CTR offset initialization could cause one agent's registration to corrupt another's. The existing duplicate-init test only covers the same-agent-id case.

**Suggested test approach**:
Spawn 3-5 concurrent init requests with distinct agent_ids using `tokio::join!` or `futures::join_all`. After all complete, verify each agent is independently registered with the correct key/IV, and that the correct number of AgentNew events were broadcast.
close_reason: Added concurrent multi-agent init stress test: 5 agents init concurrently via join_all, verifies independent registration with correct key/IV, no duplicates, and exactly 5 AgentNew events broadcast

title: test: dispatch/network.rs format_net_sessions and friends lack empty-row-data edge cases
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/network.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

The formatting functions `format_net_sessions`, `format_net_shares`, and `format_net_group_descriptions` are well-tested for normal cases, but none test rows with empty-string values. In a real callback, an agent could return empty hostnames, usernames, share names, or descriptions. The column-width calculation uses `.len()` which would return 0 for empty strings, potentially producing misaligned output if the min-width guard is insufficient.

For `format_net_sessions`: test a row with ("", "", 0, 0) to verify header alignment holds.
For `format_net_shares`: test a row with ("", "", "", 0).

Additionally, `int_to_ipv4` has good tests but the `handle_net_callback` integration tests for `Sessions` and `Share` never test the zero-rows (empty list) case — only non-empty lists.

**Suggested test approach**:
Add format function unit tests with all-empty-string rows. Add `handle_net_callback` tests for Sessions and Share with zero data rows (just the target, no loop iterations) to verify the output message contains count [0].
close_reason: Added 6 new tests: empty-string-value edge cases for format_net_sessions, format_net_shares, format_net_group_descriptions (unit), plus integration tests for Sessions/Share with empty-string row data via handle_net_callback. Discovered that format_net_group_descriptions trims all-whitespace data rows — documented in test.

title: test: assembly dispatch lacks malformed/truncated BOF payload rejection tests
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/assembly_dispatch.rs (testing teamserver/src/dispatch/assembly.rs)
**Gap type**: error path

The 7 existing assembly dispatch integration tests all send well-formed BOF callback payloads. There are no tests for:
1. A BOF_CALLBACK_OUTPUT with a length prefix that exceeds the remaining payload bytes (truncated string)
2. A payload shorter than the 4-byte sub-type header
3. A BOF_EXCEPTION payload with fewer than 12 bytes (missing address field)
4. An unknown sub-type code (e.g. 0xFF)

Without these tests, the dispatch handler could panic on malformed agent data rather than returning a clean error or logging a warning. A compromised or buggy agent sending garbage BOF responses should never crash the teamserver.

**Suggested test approach**:
Reuse the existing `start_server` + `register_agent` pattern but send truncated payloads via `valid_demon_callback_body`. Assert the teamserver either broadcasts an error-type `AgentResponse` or silently drops the callback without crashing (no panic, subsequent requests still work).
close_reason: Added 4 integration tests for malformed/truncated BOF payloads: sub-type header too short, truncated output string, BOF_EXCEPTION missing address, and unknown sub-type 0xFF. All verify no crash, proper error handling, and server recovery.

title: test: api.rs lacks test for disabled API rejecting all authenticated requests
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/api.rs
**Gap type**: error path

When the REST API is disabled (`ApiRuntime::enabled() == false`), the `authenticate` method should reject all requests because there are no registered API keys. The existing tests cover the enabled path thoroughly (auth, RBAC, rate limiting), but there is no test verifying that when the API is disabled:
1. `ApiRuntime::enabled()` returns `false`
2. Any request with an `X-Api-Key` header still gets rejected (not silently accepted)
3. The response body indicates the API is disabled or returns `invalid_api_key`

Without this test, a regression that accidentally accepts requests when the API is disabled could expose the teamserver to unauthenticated access.

**Suggested test approach**:
Build a test router with `None` (no API config), send a request with an arbitrary `X-Api-Key` header to a protected endpoint like `/listeners`, and assert the response is 401 with `invalid_api_key`.
close_reason: Added test disabled_api_rejects_authenticated_request verifying that when API is disabled (no config), requests with X-Api-Key header to /listeners return 503 with api_disabled error code

title: test: agents.rs remove/mark_dead/set_note error paths for unknown agent IDs untested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/agents.rs
**Gap type**: error path

The `remove`, `mark_dead`, and `set_note` methods on `AgentRegistry` all return `Result<_, TeamserverError>` and should return `AgentNotFound` when called with a non-existent agent ID. However, none of these error paths have dedicated tests. Only `set_last_call_in` has an explicit `returns_agent_not_found_for_unknown_id` test.

Without these tests, a refactor could silently change the error behavior (e.g., returning Ok(()) instead of Err) and break callers that rely on the error to detect missing agents.

**Suggested test approach**:
Call `remove(0xFFFF_FFFF)`, `mark_dead(0xFFFF_FFFF, "reason")`, and `set_note(0xFFFF_FFFF, "note")` on an empty registry and assert each returns `Err(TeamserverError::AgentNotFound { .. })`.
close_reason: Added three tests covering AgentNotFound error paths for remove(), mark_dead(), and set_note() on AgentRegistry with unknown agent IDs

title: test: HTTP listener pipeline lacks oversized body rejection test
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/http_listener_pipeline.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

The malformed body test (`http_listener_rejects_malformed_and_truncated_bodies`) covers empty, short, wrong magic, and truncated payloads — but does not test what happens with an excessively large body (e.g. 10 MB or 100 MB POST). For a C2 listener exposed to the network, this is a potential DoS vector if the server reads the entire body into memory before validating it.

Without this test, a regression that removes body-size limits or buffering caps would not be caught, and an adversary (or a bug in the agent) could exhaust teamserver memory with crafted oversized requests.

**Suggested test approach**:
Send a POST with a body much larger than any valid Demon envelope (e.g. 5 MB of zeros). Assert the server either returns 413 Payload Too Large or 404 (depending on design), does NOT register an agent, and remains responsive to a subsequent valid DEMON_INIT.
close_reason: Added http_listener_rejects_oversized_body test: sends MAX_AGENT_MESSAGE_LEN+1 bytes with valid magic, asserts 404 rejection, no agent registration, and listener health afterward.

title: test: add common crate integration smoke tests for public reexports
description:
**File**: common/src/lib.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- Module has no #[cfg(test)] coverage and the common crate currently has no common/tests/*.rs integration tests.
- Public API surface re-exported from red_cell_common (for example CommonError, ListenerProtocol, HttpListenerConfig, and related shared types) is only exercised from unit tests inside implementation modules.

**Why this matters**: crate consumers depend on the re-exported API, not the internal module layout. A broken re-export, visibility regression, or cross-module serialization/parsing mismatch would not be caught by unit tests confined to the source modules.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: import shared types from the crate root and round-trip representative config/domain values.
2. Error path: a crate-root import path can construct and match CommonError variants from failed parsing.
3. Edge case: integration tests compile against crate-root exports without reaching into private modules.

**Test location**: common/tests/public_api.rs
close_reason: Added common crate integration smoke tests for crate-root listener reexports and CommonError matching.

title: Teamserver - expose persisted operators and presence state
description: Extend the authentication/RBAC work so operator APIs and websocket session bootstrap can enumerate all configured and runtime-created operators, including offline presence and role metadata. Current runtime operator persistence exists, but operator discovery still centers on active sessions only.
close_reason: Exposed persisted and configured operators through REST and websocket bootstrap with presence metadata.

title: Client Python - implement active havocui tabs and Demon command tasking
description: The client-side Havoc compatibility layer now exposes havoc/havocui/Event/Demon shims and supports Havoc-style RegisterCommand plus richer command contexts, but two compatibility gaps remain for real script ports: (1) havocui tab helpers are currently output-only shims rather than real custom UI surfaces, and (2) the Demon compatibility object does not yet queue console/task commands back through the client transport. Finish those behaviors so more existing Havoc client scripts can run unmodified.
close_reason: Implemented active havocui tabs with refreshable layouts and queued Demon compatibility commands through the client transport.

title: test: add unit coverage for install_default_crypto_provider
description:
**File**: common/src/tls.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- fn install_default_crypto_provider (line 98) — installs the rustls aws-lc-rs crypto provider once for the current process before TLS identities are used.

**Why this matters**: this is process-wide initialization for every TLS listener path. If the once-only guard regresses or the provider install path stops being idempotent, TLS setup can fail only after repeated initialization or under parallel test/server startup.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: first call completes without panicking and TLS identity creation still succeeds afterward.
2. Error path: repeated calls remain idempotent and do not double-install or panic.
3. Edge case: calling it before building a rustls server config still allows TlsIdentity::server_config() to succeed.

**Test location**: inline #[cfg(test)] in common/src/tls.rs
close_reason: Added inline unit coverage for default rustls crypto provider installation paths.

title: test: SOCKS5 negotiate_socks5 lacks test for zero-method client greeting
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/sockets.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

The existing `negotiate_socks5` tests cover wrong-version (v4) and auth-only methods, but do not test a client greeting with `nmethods=0` (a valid SOCKS5 packet: `[0x05, 0x00]`). This is an edge case where the method list is empty — the server should respond with `0xFF` (no acceptable method) and return an error.

A missing check for this edge case could cause the server to accept a connection with no negotiated method, potentially allowing unauthenticated relay.

**Suggested test approach**:
Send `[0x05, 0x00]` (version 5, zero methods) to `negotiate_socks5` and assert it returns an `io::Error` with kind `InvalidData` or writes the `0xFF` rejection byte.
close_reason: Test now exists: negotiate_socks5_rejects_zero_methods (sockets.rs:1322)

title: test: add unit tests for process table formatters (format_process_table, process_rows_json, format_memory_protect)
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/process.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- `fn format_process_table` (line 416) — renders process list as aligned table; column widths are dynamic
- `fn process_rows_json` (line 449) — serializes process rows to serde_json Value array
- `fn format_module_table` (line 490) — renders loaded module table with hex base addresses
- `fn format_grep_table` (line 506) — renders process grep results table
- `fn format_memory_table` (line 532) — renders memory region table with hex addresses and protection strings
- `fn format_memory_protect` (line 560) — maps Windows memory protection constants to string names
- `fn format_memory_state` (line 586) — maps Windows memory state constants (MEM_COMMIT etc.)
- `fn format_memory_type` (line 595) — maps Windows memory type constants (MEM_PRIVATE etc.)
- `fn win32_error_code_name` (line 575) — maps Win32 error codes to symbolic names

**Why this matters**: process_rows_json feeds the operator UI's process browser. format_memory_protect with unknown constants silently falls back to hex — but known constants must be named correctly for analysts to triage injection targets.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. format_process_table: empty → empty string; one row → header + separator + row
2. process_rows_json: two rows → JSON array with correct keys/values
3. format_memory_protect: 0x20 → "PAGE_EXECUTE_READ"; 0x99 → "0x99"
4. format_memory_state: 0x1000 → "MEM_COMMIT"; unknown → hex fallback
5. win32_error_code_name: 5 → Some("ERROR_ACCESS_DENIED"); 0 → None

**Test location**: inline `#[cfg(test)]` in teamserver/src/dispatch/process.rs
close_reason: Added inline #[cfg(test)] module covering all 9 functions: format_process_table (empty+single row+dynamic width), process_rows_json (empty+two rows), format_module_table, format_grep_table, format_memory_table, format_memory_protect (all 9 constants + hex fallback), format_memory_state, format_memory_type, win32_error_code_name

title: plugins test panics: Tokio runtime shutting down during Python GIL call
description:
crates/teamserver/src/plugins.rs — test initialize_exposes_agent_and_listener_accessors fails with:
  'A Tokio 1.x context was found, but it is being shutdown.'

Root cause: the test uses std::thread::spawn to call Python::with_gil, but the Python API functions (get_agent, get_listener) internally call Handle::block_on() using the Handle captured at PluginRuntime::initialize() time. By the time the OS thread executes the GIL block, the #[tokio::test] runtime may already be shutting down, causing the block_on() to panic.

Fix: replace std::thread::spawn with tokio::task::spawn_blocking (consistent with the production code in PluginRuntime::initialize), or restructure the test so the Tokio runtime is alive for the duration of the Python call.
close_reason: Resolved while implementing the embedded PyO3 runtime: plugin tests were stabilized and pivot dispatch now uses a shared context, so cargo test and cargo clippy both pass again.

title: test: havoc_compatibility — multi-session reconnect and non-zero CTR offset cross-validation
description:
**File**: `teamserver/tests/havoc_compatibility.rs`
**Gap**: Only one test exists (`red_cell_packets_match_havoc_at_offset_zero_and_advance_afterward`). It verifies compatibility starting at CTR offset 0. There is no test that:
- Simulates a reconnect where the CTR offset is > 0 and verifies that the Red Cell teamserver and the reference Havoc implementation still agree on the keystream.
- Verifies compatibility for the DEMON_INFO callback packet (beyond DEMON_INIT).

**Why this matters**: AES-CTR two-time-pad is a critical security property. A regression that resets the offset to 0 on reconnect would pass the offset-zero test but fail in production. Cross-validating against the Go reference implementation at a non-trivial offset catches drift between the Rust and Go crypto paths.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Advance the CTR offset past at least one full AES block (16 bytes) by encrypting N messages.
2. Capture the expected next-block ciphertext using the Havoc Go reference.
3. Assert that Red Cell produces the identical ciphertext for that next message.

**Test location**: `teamserver/tests/havoc_compatibility.rs`
close_reason: Added havoc-compat coverage for DEMON_INFO callback ciphertext and reconnect ACK keystream at non-zero CTR offsets.

title: test: byte_count boundary values at unit transitions and u64::MAX untested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/transfer.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

`byte_count()` has tests for exact unit boundaries (0, 999, 1000, 1M, 1T) but not for values *near* boundaries or extreme values. Specifically untested:
- 999_999 (should display as "1000.00 kB" — verify no off-by-one in unit selection)
- 999_999_999_999_999 (should reach TB correctly)
- u64::MAX (overflow risk in f64 conversion — verify no panic)

Since this function formats download sizes shown to operators, incorrect output could mislead about exfiltration volumes.

**Suggested test approach**:
Add assertions for `byte_count(999_999)`, `byte_count(u64::MAX)`, and a value that exercises rounding near the GB→TB boundary.

title: test: listener_lifecycle.rs missing update-while-running behavior test
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/listener_lifecycle.rs
**Gap type**: existence

The listener lifecycle tests cover create → start → stop → restart → delete transitions, port conflicts, crash recovery, and concurrent operations. However, there is no test for updating a listener's configuration while it is in the Running state.

`ListenerManager::update()` is used in `e2e_operator_agent_session.rs` but only as a setup step — the test doesn't assert that the update actually changed the persisted config or affected runtime behavior. A regression in update-while-running could silently discard config changes or leave the runtime out of sync with the database.

**Suggested test approach**:
Create and start a listener on port A. Call `manager.update()` with a modified config (e.g., different host_rotation or user_agent). Assert: (1) `manager.summary()` reflects the new config, (2) the listener is still in Running state, (3) the DB persisted the update.
close_reason: Added update_running_listener_persists_non_port_config_changes test that verifies host_rotation and user_agent changes are persisted and reflected in summary while listener stays Running

title: test: dispatch/util basename has no tests for edge-case path inputs
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/util.rs
**Gap type**: edge case / existence

`basename` (line 66) is used in `checkin.rs` to extract the agent process name from the full executable path reported by the agent. It splits on both `\\` and `/` but has no unit tests at all.

Untested cases that could produce wrong process names visible in the operator console:
- Empty string input → should return empty string (currently returns `path` which is also empty; worth asserting)
- Path with no separator (bare filename) → should return the filename unchanged
- Windows path with only backslash separators: `C:\\Windows\\system32\\cmd.exe` → should return `cmd.exe`
- POSIX path with forward slash: `/usr/bin/ls` → should return `ls`
- Mixed separators: `C:\\path/to\\file.exe` → should return `file.exe`
- Trailing separator: `C:\\Windows\\` → should return empty string (rsplit returns empty after final separator)

**Suggested test approach**:
Add a `#[cfg(test)]` module in `util.rs` with `#[test]` cases covering all path forms above, asserting the exact return value for each.
close_reason: Added 9 unit tests covering all edge cases for basename: empty string, bare filename, Windows backslash, POSIX forward slash, mixed separators, trailing separators, single separator, and root slash.

title: test: add unit tests for AuthenticationFailure::message() and login_failure_message() with SessionCapExceeded
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/auth.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- `AuthenticationFailure::message()` (line 105) — only tested indirectly via `login_failure_message` with `InvalidCredentials`; the `SessionCapExceeded` branch ("Too many active sessions; try again later") has no test
- `login_failure_message()` (line 473) — line 1268 tests with `InvalidCredentials` only; `SessionCapExceeded` variant never exercised

**Why this matters**: These messages are part of the Havoc-compatible wire protocol sent to the operator client. If someone renames or changes the `SessionCapExceeded` text (e.g., during auth refactoring), the client receives the wrong error string and there is no test to catch the regression.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: `AuthenticationFailure::SessionCapExceeded.message()` returns the expected static string.
2. Happy path: `login_failure_message("user", &AuthenticationFailure::SessionCapExceeded)` returns an `InitConnectionError` variant with the cap-exceeded text in the body.
3. Coverage check: assert both variants of `AuthenticationFailure` have non-empty message strings (guards against future variants being missed).

**Test location**: inline `#[cfg(test)]` in teamserver/src/auth.rs, alongside the existing `login_success_message_uses_init_connection_success_wire_shape` test
close_reason: Added three tests: SessionCapExceeded.message() return value, login_failure_message with SessionCapExceeded wire shape, and all-variants non-empty guard

title: test: add unit test for AppState::update_agent_note
description:
**File**: `client/src/transport.rs`
**Functions with no tests**:
- `AppState::update_agent_note` (line 718) — finds an agent by `name_id` in `self.agents` and updates its `note` field; silently no-ops if the agent is not found

**Why this matters**: Notes are the only way for operators to annotate agents in the UI. A regression (wrong field updated, wrong agent matched) would silently produce confusing behavior.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: insert an agent, call `update_agent_note(agent_id, "my note")`, assert `agent.note == "my note"`
2. Note update: update the note a second time, assert the new value replaces the old
3. Unknown agent: call with an unknown ID, assert no panic and agents list unchanged

**Test location**: inline `#[cfg(test)]` in `client/src/transport.rs`
close_reason: Added inline transport unit test covering note set, note replacement, and unknown-agent no-op behavior.

title: DNS pending responses not expired if agent dies mid-download
description:
Introduced by: Codex

crates/teamserver/src/listeners.rs:1337 — DnsListenerState.responses (HashMap<u32, DnsPendingResponse>) is only cleaned up in handle_download when the seq index goes past the end of the chunk list (line 1470). There is no timestamp on DnsPendingResponse, so if an agent requests seq=0 then dies, its response entry remains in the map indefinitely.

The cleanup_expired_uploads task only prunes the 'uploads' map; it ignores 'responses'.

This is distinct from the previously closed red-cell-c2-2jw (which fixed the case where responses were never removed after full retrieval). The residual case is partial retrieval: agent starts download at seq=0 but the connection drops.

Fix: add a received_at timestamp to DnsPendingResponse and include response map cleanup in cleanup_expired_uploads, using the same DNS_UPLOAD_TIMEOUT_SECS threshold or a separate configurable TTL.
close_reason: Already implemented on main: DNS pending responses now carry received_at timestamps, cleanup prunes expired responses, and the regression tests pass.

title: test: shutdown coordinator in main.rs lacks end-to-end coverage
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/main.rs
**Gap type**: integration / error path

`wait_for_shutdown_signal` coordinates several shutdown-critical side effects: initiating `ShutdownController`, triggering Axum graceful shutdown, waiting for listener callback drain, waiting for audit webhooks, and closing the database. None of those behaviors are exercised by a test in this file.

Without coverage here, a regression in shutdown ordering or timeout handling could leave the teamserver hanging on exit, skip database close, or stop surfacing the warning paths when callbacks/webhooks fail to drain. The existing unit tests around `ShutdownController` do not protect the orchestration logic in `main.rs`.

**Suggested test approach**:
Add an integration-style test around the shutdown coordinator, either by factoring the signal wait behind a test seam or by driving a subprocess/server instance to shutdown. Assert that shutdown completes, the database is closed, and the callback-drain / webhook-timeout branches are observable when collaborators report `false`.
close_reason: Extracted run_shutdown_sequence from wait_for_shutdown_signal and added 4 tests covering: database close, shutdown controller state, clean shutdown with no listeners, and shutdown with an active HTTP listener draining before database close.

title: Implement Demon binary protocol types
description: In crates/common: define all Rust structs and enums for the Demon binary C2 protocol. Packet header: Size(u32), Magic(u32)=0xDEADBEEF, AgentID(u32). All command IDs as enum: DEMON_INIT(99), COMMAND_CHECKIN(100), COMMAND_GET_JOB(1), COMMAND_PROC(0x1010), COMMAND_INJECT_DLL(22), COMMAND_TOKEN(40), COMMAND_KERBEROS(2550), COMMAND_SCREENSHOT(2510), COMMAND_SOCKET(2540), etc. Callback types: CALLBACK_OUTPUT, CALLBACK_ERROR, CALLBACK_FILE. Implement binary serialization/deserialization (byteorder or binrw crate). Write unit tests for round-trip encoding.
close_reason: Implemented Havoc-compatible Demon protocol types, enums, serializers, and tests

title: Implement file browser panel
description: In crates/client: file browser UI for navigating agent file system. Tree view with lazy-load (expand directory triggers ls command). File actions: download, upload (file picker dialog), delete. Show file size, timestamps, permissions. Download progress bar. Integration with loot panel (downloaded files appear in loot).
close_reason: Implemented the client file browser panel with lazy directory loading, filesystem actions, and download progress/loot integration.

title: Integration tests: mock Demon agent checkin
description: Integration tests that spin up a real teamserver (in-process, ephemeral SQLite) and simulate a Demon agent. Test flow: start teamserver, connect mock agent over HTTP, send DEMON_INIT packet (properly encrypted with test key), verify AgentNew event emitted, send COMMAND_GET_JOB, enqueue a job via WebSocket, verify agent receives job, send CALLBACK_OUTPUT, verify operator receives result.
close_reason: Added an integration test that exercises Demon init, job polling, and callback output through the real listener and websocket stack.

title: test: parse_callback_packages multi-package chaining path is untested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/demon.rs
**Gap type**: error path / edge case

`parse_callback_packages` reads the first package from the decrypted payload, then continues in a loop to parse additional (command_id, request_id, length-prefixed payload) tuples until the buffer is exhausted. The multi-package path (the `while offset < decrypted.len()` loop at line 223) has **no test** — every existing test exercises single-package callbacks only.

A bug in the multi-package loop (e.g., off-by-one on the remaining-bytes check, wrong field ordering) would silently drop or misattribute additional packages from a single callback response. This matters because Demon can piggyback multiple output events in one HTTP response.

**Suggested test approach**:
Build a callback packet whose decrypted payload encodes two or three packages (different command IDs and request IDs), parse it with `DemonPacketParser`, and assert that `ParsedDemonPacket::Callback { packages }` contains all expected packages in order with correct `command_id`, `request_id`, and `payload` fields.
close_reason: Added 5 unit tests for parse_callback_packages covering the multi-package while loop: 3-package chaining, single-package (loop not entered), empty first payload, truncated command_id error, truncated payload error

title: test: client local_config.rs lacks tests for corrupt/malformed TOML input
description:
Introduced by: Claude Opus 4.6

client/src/local_config.rs:43 — load_from() uses toml::from_str().unwrap_or_default() which silently falls back to default config on parse failure. While this is acceptable for user convenience, there are no tests verifying:
1. That corrupt TOML files produce the default config (not a partial config)  
2. That partially valid TOML with some fields present but others malformed produces correct defaults for the bad fields
3. That TOML injection via crafted server_url or username values is handled safely
close_reason: Added 10 tests covering corrupt TOML (binary garbage, truncated, duplicate keys, wrong types, mixed valid/invalid), TOML injection via crafted server_url and username, extremely long values, and unknown field handling

title: test: filesystem dispatch Remove/Mkdir/Copy/Move subcommands have no tests
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/filesystem.rs
**Gap type**: existence

The `Remove` (line 280), `Mkdir` (line 293), and `Copy`/`Move` (line 304) subcommand handlers parse success/failure status and path strings from agent callbacks, but none have any test coverage.

These handlers share a common pattern: read a success u32, read a path/message string, then broadcast a Good or Error event. A regression in the success/failure branching logic would go completely undetected.

The `Copy`/`Move` handler has additional logic to select the right verb ("copied" vs "moved") based on the subcommand — this conditional is untested.

**Suggested test approach**:
For each subcommand: (1) test success path — payload with success=1 and a path, assert broadcast contains "Good" and the path. (2) Test failure path — success=0, assert broadcast contains "Error". (3) Test truncated payload returns `InvalidCallbackPayload`. For Copy/Move, verify the verb matches the subcommand ID.
close_reason: duplicate of red-cell-c2-ggap (subset: Remove/Mkdir/Copy/Move already covered by ggap)

title: test: pivot SmbCommand forwarding and dispatch_builtin_packages lack test coverage
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/pivot.rs
**Functions with no tests**:
- `handle_pivot_command_callback` (line 213) — parses nested callback package from child agent, updates last_call_in timestamp, delegates to `dispatch_builtin_packages`
- `dispatch_builtin_packages` (line 243) — creates a fresh CommandDispatcher, registers builtin handlers, and collects response bytes for forwarding back through the pivot chain

**Why this matters**: This is the data forwarding path for SMB pivoted agents. Every command response from a child agent flows through these two functions. A bug here would silently drop or corrupt agent output for all pivoted sessions — a critical operational failure that existing tests cannot detect.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Happy path: valid nested callback package → child agent last_call_in updated, inner command dispatched, response bytes collected
2. Error path: nested package is not a callback envelope → InvalidCallbackPayload
3. Error path: nested package parse failure → InvalidCallbackPayload with protocol error
4. Round-trip: dispatch_builtin_packages with a simple CommandOutput package → verifies the recursive dispatch actually emits events

**Test location**: `teamserver/tests/pivot_dispatch.rs` or inline `#[cfg(test)]` in pivot.rs
close_reason: Added 4 unit tests in pivot.rs covering handle_pivot_command_callback (happy path, non-callback envelope error, truncated payload error) and dispatch_builtin_packages (CommandOutput round-trip with event verification)

title: security: AES-256-CTR keystream reused for every agent message (two-time-pad)
description:
Introduced by: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

common/src/crypto.rs — encrypt_agent_data always calls encrypt_agent_data_at_offset with block_offset=0, resetting the keystream to the start for every message. With a fixed per-agent key+IV pair and offset 0 per message, the keystream is identical for every message. Any two messages encrypted with the same agent key XOR to the XOR of their plaintexts — a classic two-time-pad attack.

An attacker recording C2 traffic who can cause the agent to encrypt a known value (e.g., a crafted command output) can recover other plaintexts. This is an inherited Havoc protocol constraint, but operators must be warned that CTR-per-message confidentiality is weaker than an AEAD scheme.

Fix: document the known limitation explicitly in crypto.rs; consider adding a per-message nonce prefix to the IV when Havoc compatibility is not required, or at minimum add an operator-facing warning to the profile docs.
close_reason: Documented two-time-pad limitation in crypto.rs module docs and per-function Security sections; created docs/operator-security.md with mitigation guidance for operators using Havoc-compatible vs custom transports

title: Timing attack: password hash comparison uses non-constant-time eq_ignore_ascii_case
description:
Introduced by: Codex <noreply@openai.com>

crates/teamserver/src/auth.rs:135 — The login handler compares SHA3-256 password hashes with `eq_ignore_ascii_case`, which is a variable-time string comparison. An attacker making many login attempts can use response timing to deduce partial information about the stored hash, enabling a timing-based oracle attack.

Fix: replace with `subtle::ConstantTimeEq` or compare the lower-hex digests with a constant-time byte comparison. The `subtle` crate is already used in the dependency tree via ring. At minimum convert both sides to lowercase and use `subtle::ConstantTimeEq` on the byte slices.
close_reason: Replaced variable-time password hash comparison with a constant-time comparison and added coverage for uppercase SHA3 digests.

title: test: build_payload rejects wrong agent type and invalid arch/format — paths untested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/payload_builder.rs
**Gap type**: error path

`PayloadBuilderService::build_payload` has three early-exit error paths that are completely untested:

1. `request.agent_type.trim() != "Demon"` — returns `PayloadBuildError::InvalidRequest`; no test passes a non-Demon agent type (e.g. "Shellcode", "CobaltStrike")
2. `Architecture::parse` — returns error for unknown arch strings (e.g. "arm64", "aarch64"); this is unreachable by tested code paths
3. `OutputFormat::parse` — returns error for unknown format strings (e.g. "Linux Elf"); untested

Additionally, only the x64 architecture path is exercised. The x86 path uses a different compiler binary (`compiler_x86`) and `nasm_format` returns "win32" — a bug in the x86 compile path would not be caught.

**Suggested test approach**:
Using `PayloadBuilderService::disabled_for_tests()` or a stub toolchain, call `build_payload` with agent_type="Shellcode", arch="arm64", and format="Linux Elf" and assert each returns the correct `PayloadBuildError::InvalidRequest` variant. Add an x86 compile path test using the mock toolchain from the existing integration test.
close_reason: Added coverage for invalid build requests and x86 payload compilation path

title: test: screenshot dispatch handler lacks inline unit tests — all coverage depends on E2E integration
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/screenshot.rs
**Gap type**: existence

`handle_screenshot_callback` has zero inline unit tests. All test coverage comes from `teamserver/tests/screenshot_dispatch.rs`, which exercises the function through the full HTTP→listener→dispatch pipeline. This is expensive (~200ms per test) and brittle — a change to the listener pipeline could break these tests even if the screenshot logic itself is correct.

The function has three meaningful branches (success=0, success=1 with empty bytes, success=1 with data) plus loot persistence and plugin emission. A unit test with a mocked database and event bus would catch regressions faster and with better isolation.

**Suggested test approach**:
Create inline `#[cfg(test)]` tests that call `handle_screenshot_callback` directly with a real Database::connect_in_memory(), a fresh AgentRegistry/EventBus, and None for plugins. Test: (1) success path stores loot and broadcasts two events, (2) success=0 broadcasts error and stores no loot, (3) success=1 with empty bytes broadcasts error.
close_reason: Added 5 inline unit tests to screenshot.rs covering success (loot + 2 events), failure (success=0), empty bytes, and 2 truncated payload variants. Tests run in ~30ms with in-memory DB vs ~200ms E2E.

title: test: extract shared TeamserverState setup into common test helper to reduce duplication
description:
**Files**: teamserver/tests/mock_demon_agent_checkin.rs, teamserver/tests/output_dispatch.rs, teamserver/tests/screenshot_dispatch.rs, teamserver/tests/e2e_operator_agent_session.rs, teamserver/tests/socks5_relay.rs
**Gap type**: quality

The TeamserverState construction (Profile::parse → Database → AgentRegistry → EventBus → ListenerManager → TeamserverState → TcpListener::bind → tokio::spawn) is duplicated nearly verbatim in 5+ test files and 10+ individual test functions. This ~30-line boilerplate block makes tests harder to read and means any API change to TeamserverState requires updating every test.

Several files (output_dispatch.rs, screenshot_dispatch.rs) already have a local `start_server()` helper, but each defines its own version. These should be unified into `common/mod.rs` (which already exists and provides other helpers).

**Suggested approach**:
Add a `spawn_test_server_with_listeners(Profile) -> (SocketAddr, ListenerManager, AgentRegistry, EventBus, Database)` function to `teamserver/tests/common/mod.rs`. Migrate the per-file `start_server()` helpers to call it.
close_reason: Expanded TestServer with database/sockets fields, added register_agent and default_test_profile helpers in common/mod.rs, migrated all 5 test files (output_dispatch, screenshot_dispatch, socks5_relay, e2e_operator_agent_session, mock_demon_agent_checkin) to use the shared spawn_test_server. Net -251 lines.

title: test: mock_demon_agent_checkin — multi-output streaming for a single task
description:
**File**: `teamserver/tests/mock_demon_agent_checkin.rs`
**Gap**: The two existing tests cover single-output task flow and reconnect synchronisation. There is no test for a task that produces multiple sequential callback packets (streaming/interactive output), verifying that all chunks reach the operator event bus in order and are attributed to the same task ID.

**Why this matters**: Interactive commands (shell, powershell) generate multiple output packets per task. If the output-assembly or task-attribution logic is broken, operators would see truncated or mis-attributed results. This scenario also exercises the event bus back-pressure path more thoroughly.

**Functions under test**: HTTP callback handler → `handle_demon_info_callback` / output dispatcher → `EventBus::publish` (multiple times for one task) → operator message assembly.

**Minimum test scenarios needed**:
1. Enqueue one task, send three separate callback packets with the same task ID, verify three `AgentResponse` events arrive on the event bus with correct sequence.
2. Enqueue two tasks, interleave callbacks for both tasks, verify correct attribution for each.

**Test location**: `teamserver/tests/mock_demon_agent_checkin.rs`
close_reason: Added streaming and interleaved mock demon checkin coverage for multi-packet task output.

title: security: Argon2 password hashing uses unconfigured default parameters
description:
Introduced by: Codex <noreply@openai.com>

teamserver/src/auth.rs:516,527,605 — All password hashing and verification calls use `Argon2::default()` without explicit parameter configuration. The argon2 0.6.x crate defaults to m_cost=19456 (19 MiB), t_cost=2, p_cost=1. While these meet OWASP's minimum threshold, they're at the bottom of the recommendation range.

For a C2 teamserver that stores operator credentials, stronger parameters are warranted: m_cost=65536 (64 MiB), t_cost=3, p_cost=4 (OWASP's recommended configuration). The fix is to construct `Argon2::new(Algorithm::Argon2id, Version::V0x13, Params::new(65536, 3, 4, None).unwrap())` and use it consistently.

This is not exploitable today but reduces the safety margin against offline brute-force if the database is exfiltrated.
close_reason: Replaced all Argon2::default() calls with argon2_hasher() using OWASP-recommended Argon2id parameters (m=65536, t=3, p=4). Updated e2e test timeouts to accommodate stronger hashing.

title: test: handle_pivot_list_callback lacks unit tests for empty and multi-entry formatting
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/pivot.rs
**Gap type**: existence

`handle_pivot_list_callback` formats a table of connected pivot agents (DemonID + named pipe). It has two code paths — empty list ("No pivots connected") and populated list with hex-formatted agent IDs. Neither path has a unit test. The only pivot.rs tests cover `inner_demon_agent_id`. Existing issue red-cell-c2-34bb covers disconnect/malformed envelopes but not the list handler.

A formatting bug (e.g., wrong hex width, missing newlines) would produce garbled output in the operator console with no test to catch it.

**Suggested test approach**:
1. Empty parser (no entries): assert broadcasts "No pivots connected" with Type=Info.
2. Two entries: build payload with two (u32 demon_id, utf16 pipe_name) pairs, assert output contains both hex IDs and pipe names in table format.
3. Truncated entry (demon_id present but pipe name missing): assert returns InvalidCallbackPayload.
close_reason: Duplicate of red-cell-c2-yw6v — tests already added in commit 0bac188

title: test: listener restart (stop then re-start same listener) has no coverage
description:
Introduced by: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context)

teamserver/tests/listener_lifecycle.rs — Tests cover create/start/stop/update/clear but never exercise the stop-then-restart path for a single listener. This is a common operational pattern (e.g. rotating TLS certs) that could regress silently. Should verify port rebinding, agent reconnection, and state consistency.
close_reason: Added 4 tests covering stop-then-restart: port rebinding verification, multiple restart cycles, agent reconnection after restart, and config preservation across restart cycles

title: sockets.rs cleanup paths silently swallow errors without logging
description:
Introduced by: Codex (teamserver/src/sockets.rs — see red-cell-c2-hctp)

This duplicates existing issue red-cell-c2-hctp. Confirmed the pattern still exists:
teamserver/src/sockets.rs:352,364,415-416,425,431-432,537-538 — all use let _ = to
discard remove_client, enqueue_close_job, and writer.shutdown() results.

Already tracked as red-cell-c2-hctp.
close_reason: Duplicate of existing red-cell-c2-hctp

title: teamserver: trusted proxy config for X-Forwarded-For in HTTP listener
description: The red-cell-c2-3nf bug tracks that external_ip is always 0.0.0.0. The proper fix requires a profile-level behind_redirector flag (already partially present) and a trusted_proxies CIDR list. When behind_redirector=true and source IP is in trusted_proxies, use X-Forwarded-For as the agent IP. Otherwise use socket peer address directly. Prevents IP spoofing when the flag is off.
close_reason: trusted proxy X-Forwarded-For parsing implemented in listeners.rs resolve_client_ip() with behind_redirector flag and trusted_proxy_peers CIDR list

title: test: socks5_relay has no coverage for operations on nonexistent socket_id
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/socks5_relay.rs
**Gap type**: error path

The relay tests verify `finish_connect` and `write_client_data` for valid socket IDs but never test what happens when these functions are called with a socket_id that does not exist in the relay's connected-socket table. In production, a buggy or replayed agent callback could reference a stale or fabricated socket_id. Without this test, a panic or unbounded map growth could go undetected.

**Suggested test approach**:
1. Call `relay.finish_connect(agent_id, 0xDEAD, true, 0)` with a socket_id that was never created. Assert it returns an error (not a panic).
2. Call `relay.write_client_data(agent_id, 0xDEAD, b"data")` with a nonexistent socket_id. Assert it returns an error.
close_reason: Tests now exist: write_client_data_returns_client_not_found_for_unknown_agent/socket, close_client_returns_client_not_found_for_unknown_agent/socket (sockets.rs:1007-1073)

title: test: windows_version_label has zero test coverage despite 11-branch Havoc-compatibility logic
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/util.rs
**Gap type**: existence

`windows_version_label` is a pure function with 11 conditional branches that maps (major, minor, product_type, service_pack, build) tuples to human-readable OS strings matching the original Havoc teamserver exactly. It is used in both the DEMON_INIT path and the CHECKIN path to produce the agent's displayed OS version. There are currently zero tests for this function.

Potential bugs that would go undetected:
- A wrong build number range for Windows 11 (22000–22621) would silently misclassify Win11 as Win10 or vice versa.
- A wrong VER_NT_WORKSTATION comparison for the Server 2022 branch (requires exact build=20348 check) could mis-classify Windows Server.
- The service pack suffix (' Service Pack N') is only appended when service_pack != 0 — the append-nothing branch has no test.
- The `product_type != VER_NT_WORKSTATION` fallthrough to 'Windows 2016 Server' for unknown Win10 server builds has no test.

**Suggested test approach**:
Write a table-driven test over representative (major, minor, product_type, service_pack, build) inputs and assert each returns the correct string. Cover at least: Win10 workstation, Win11 (build in range), Windows Server 2022 (exact build), Server 2022 fallback, service_pack != 0 (appends suffix), and the Unknown fallback.
close_reason: Added 5 tests (17 cases) in dispatch::util::tests covering all 11 windows_version_label branches, service pack append/omit, and unknown fallback

title: test: database listener repository missing duplicate name rejection test
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/database.rs, teamserver/src/database.rs
**Gap type**: error path

The database tests thoroughly cover duplicate rejection for operators (line 177) and links (line 2297), but there is no test verifying that creating two listeners with the same name returns a UNIQUE constraint error. The `listener_repository_supports_crud_queries` test only covers the happy path.

Without this test, a regression that silently overwrites an existing listener config on duplicate create would go undetected. This matters for operational safety — accidentally overwriting a live listener's config could disrupt active agent communications.

**Suggested test approach**:
Call `repository.create(&listener)` twice with the same listener config. Assert the second call returns `TeamserverError::Database` with SQLite error code 1555 (UNIQUE constraint). Verify the original listener config is unchanged via `repository.get()`.
close_reason: consolidated into red-cell-c2-0pgqt

title: Implement WebSocket client connection to teamserver
description: In crates/client: async WebSocket client using tokio-tungstenite. Connect to wss://host:port/havoc/. Accept self-signed server certs (same as Havoc behavior). Background tokio task for receiving events and updating shared app state (Arc<Mutex<AppState>>). Sending task for queuing outgoing operator commands. Reconnection with exponential backoff. Connection status indicator in UI.
close_reason: Implemented client WebSocket transport with TLS, reconnect logic, shared state updates, and UI status.

title: test: DemonPackage::from_bytes with payload_len near u32::MAX lacks extreme allocation guard test
description:
**File**: common/src/demon.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

`DemonPackage::parse_from` reads a `payload_len` as `u32` from the wire (line 567), converts it to `usize`, then calls `read_vec` which checks `remaining < len` before allocating (line 642). Existing truncation tests use modest mismatches (declared 4, actual 2).

There is no test where `payload_len` is set to `u32::MAX` (4 GiB) with only a 12-byte buffer. While the code should safely return `BufferTooShort`, an explicit test pins this behavior and guards against future refactors that might attempt the allocation before the length check.

**Suggested test approach**:
Construct a 16-byte buffer: 4 bytes command_id + 4 bytes request_id + `u32::MAX.to_le_bytes()` as payload_len + zero trailing data. Call `DemonPackage::from_bytes` and assert it returns `BufferTooShort` with `expected = u32::MAX as usize` and `actual = 0`.
close_reason: Added test demon_package_rejects_u32_max_payload_len_without_allocating that constructs a 12-byte buffer with payload_len=u32::MAX and asserts BufferTooShort is returned without allocation

title: test: cover mismatched TLS cert/key failure paths in tls identity setup
description:
**File**: common/src/tls.rs
**Gap type**: error path

`load_tls_identity` currently accepts any syntactically valid PEM pair, and the existing tests only cover missing/corrupt PEM or successful setup. There is no test that uses a certificate and private key from different identities and then exercises `TlsIdentity::server_config()` / `TlsIdentity::tls_acceptor()`.

That means a regression could slip through where mismatched key material either panics later, returns the wrong error variant, or is only discovered deep in listener startup instead of being caught by a focused test. TLS identity handling is security-critical and this is the main real-world configuration failure mode after bad file contents.

**Suggested test approach**:
Generate two self-signed identities, combine the certificate PEM from one with the private key PEM from the other, load the pair, then assert that `server_config()` and `tls_acceptor()` fail with a rustls-backed error rather than succeeding or panicking.
close_reason: Added 3 tests covering mismatched TLS cert/key failure paths: same-algorithm ECDSA mismatch via server_config(), same via tls_acceptor(), and cross-algorithm RSA cert with ECDSA key. All assert TlsError::Rustls.

title: AES-CTR block offset lost on teamserver restart — reconnect probe sends ack at wrong counter
description:
Introduced by: Cursor (Ubuntu-HTB-cursor, working on red-cell-c2-2bm)

crates/teamserver/src/agents.rs:61,69 — AgentEntry.ctr_block_offset
crates/teamserver/src/agents.rs:110-133 — AgentRegistry::load

ctr_block_offset is initialized to Mutex::new(0) in AgentEntry::new(), which is called from AgentRegistry::load(). After a teamserver restart, all agents' CTR offsets reset to 0, but the agent processes have their counters at position N (accumulated across all prior exchanges).

Two failure modes:
1. Reconnect probe (empty DEMON_INIT): server sends build_init_ack encrypted at CTR=0 instead of the agent's expected CTR=N. Agent decrypts garbage as the ack.
2. Direct callbacks (no re-init): server tries to decrypt at CTR=0 while agent sends data encrypted at CTR=N. All decryption fails.

In the Havoc protocol agents normally re-send a full DEMON_INIT when reconnecting (which would reset CTR on both sides), but the reconnect probe path (empty DEMON_INIT body) does not trigger a full re-init and is therefore broken after a restart.

Fix: Either persist ctr_block_offset to the ts_agents table and reload on startup, or explicitly invalidate the CTR state on reconnect to force a full re-init.
close_reason: Added restart regression coverage for reconnect ack and post-restart callback parsing under restored Havoc stateless AES-CTR semantics.

title: Implement operator event broadcast system
description: In crates/teamserver: event broadcasting infrastructure. Use tokio::sync::broadcast channel to push events to all connected operator WebSocket connections. EventBus struct: subscribe() returns a Receiver, broadcast(event) sends to all. Event types from common crate. Handle slow/disconnected operators (drop lagging receivers). Write unit tests.
close_reason: Implemented teamserver event bus with broadcast subscriptions and unit tests

title: test: FingerprintCertificateVerifier verify_server_cert lacks direct unit tests for match/mismatch
description:
**File**: client/src/transport.rs
**Gap type**: error path

`FingerprintCertificateVerifier::verify_server_cert` (line 1895) is the core of the certificate pinning security feature. It:
- Computes SHA-256 of the server's end-entity certificate
- Compares it (case-insensitively) against the expected fingerprint
- Stores the actual fingerprint in the sink for UI display

There are no unit tests that exercise this verifier directly. The only test coverage is the string-level `classify_tls_error` tests that check error message formatting. A bug in the fingerprint comparison (e.g., wrong hash algorithm, byte order issue, case sensitivity) would silently accept any certificate when pinning is configured.

**Suggested test approach**:
Construct a `FingerprintCertificateVerifier` with a known fingerprint. Create a mock `CertificateDer` from a test certificate. Call `verify_server_cert` with matching and non-matching fingerprints. Assert that match succeeds and mismatch returns an error. Verify the fingerprint_sink captures the actual certificate fingerprint.
close_reason: Tests already exist: fingerprint_verifier_accepts_matching_cert, fingerprint_verifier_rejects_mismatched_cert, and fingerprint_verifier_case_insensitive_match were added in commit 9d8e1358. All 3 pass.

title: test: cover filesystem directory listing explorer and list-only branches
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/filesystem.rs
**Gap type**: edge case

`handle_filesystem_callback` has no tests for the `DemonFilesystemCommand::Dir` branch when `explorer=true`, `list_only=true`, or when the agent reports success but returns zero rows. The current test coverage only exercises download helpers and low-level beacon file parsing, so regressions in File Explorer payload encoding, path trimming, or the "No file or folder was found" fallback would slip through.

**Suggested test approach**:
Drive `CommandFs`/`Dir` through `CommandDispatcher` with payloads for explorer mode and list-only mode, then assert the operator event message, output text, and decoded `MiscData` JSON contain the expected path and row fields.
close_reason: Added 4 async tests covering Dir branch: explorer mode (MiscData JSON validation), list-only mode (path concatenation), success with zero rows, and failure case. All verify broadcast event content.

title: Client config file written without restrictive file permissions
description:
Introduced by: Claude Opus 4.6

client/src/local_config.rs:58-65 — The save_to() method writes the config file (containing server URL, username, CA cert path, certificate fingerprints) using std::fs::write() without setting restrictive file permissions. On Unix, the resulting file inherits the umask default, which is often 0644 (world-readable).

Fix: Use std::fs::OpenOptions with mode 0o600 on Unix to ensure the config is owner-read/write only. This prevents other local users from reading sensitive connection details.
close_reason: Replaced fs::write with OpenOptions+mode(0o600) and explicit set_permissions on Unix. Added tests for new-file and existing-file permission enforcement.

title: test: no E2E test for DNS listener agent registration flow
description:
Introduced by: Codex (teamserver/src/listeners.rs DNS runtime)

The DNS listener has extensive unit tests for packet parsing and protocol handling,
but there is no integration test that exercises the full flow: DNS agent init →
registration → callback → response. The HTTP and SMB listeners both have pipeline
tests (http_listener_pipeline.rs, smb_listener.rs), but DNS does not.

This is a coverage gap for a security-critical transport.
close_reason: Added dns_listener_pipeline.rs with 4 E2E tests covering DNS agent init, registration, callback, download, duplicate init rejection, and unknown agent handling

title: test: no test verifying QueueFull is returned when per-agent job queue reaches MAX_JOB_QUEUE_DEPTH
description:
Introduced by: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

teamserver/src/agents.rs — enqueue_job() enforces MAX_JOB_QUEUE_DEPTH = 1000 per agent.

There is no test that fills a queue to the limit and verifies that the next enqueue returns QueueFull instead of silently accepting the task. Without this test, a regression that removes or raises the cap would not be caught before deployment.

Fix: add a unit test that enqueues MAX_JOB_QUEUE_DEPTH tasks successfully, then asserts that task MAX_JOB_QUEUE_DEPTH+1 returns Err(AgentError::QueueFull) (or equivalent).
close_reason: test enqueue_job_returns_queue_full_at_capacity already exists in agents.rs and passes — fills queue to MAX_JOB_QUEUE_DEPTH then asserts QueueFull on the next enqueue

title: test: pivot SmbDisconnect callback lacks tests for cascading agent state updates
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/dispatch/pivot.rs (handle_pivot_disconnect_callback, lines 174–211)
**Gap type**: existence | error path

`handle_pivot_disconnect_callback` handles SMB pivot agent disconnection. It calls `registry.disconnect_link()` which cascades status updates to all transitive child agents, then broadcasts `agent_mark_event` for each affected agent. There are zero tests for this handler.

Two scenarios are untested:
1. **Success path (success != 0)**: verifies `disconnect_link` is called, affected child agents receive mark events, and the response message includes the correct child agent ID.
2. **Failure path (success == 0)**: verifies the error broadcast message and that no `disconnect_link` call occurs.

A bug in the cascading disconnect could leave child agents appearing alive after their parent pivot is disconnected, which would mislead operators about active agent connectivity.

**Suggested test approach**:
Register a parent agent with one or more child pivot agents, then send a disconnect callback. Assert: (a) the child agent's status is updated to disconnected, (b) mark events are broadcast for all affected agents, (c) the response event contains the correct child agent ID. For the failure path, assert the error broadcast and verify child agents remain unchanged.
close_reason: Added 3 tests for handle_pivot_disconnect_callback: success path with child mark, cascading grandchild disconnect, and failure path with no state change

title: security: 30 MiB body buffered pre-magic-check — memory DoS via concurrent connections
description:
Introduced by: Claude Sonnet 4.6

teamserver/src/listeners.rs:2524 (http_listener_handler)

In http_listener_handler, the sequence is:
1. http_request_matches (method, URI, user-agent, headers) — O(1) work
2. to_bytes(body, MAX_AGENT_MESSAGE_LEN) — buffers up to 30 MiB
3. is_valid_demon_callback_request — magic check

An adversary matching the HTTP listener's URI, method, and any configured User-Agent/headers (all observable from legitimate traffic) can send requests with a 30 MiB body. The server will buffer the full 30 MiB before rejecting the packet at the magic check. With no per-IP request rate limiter for callbacks (DemonInitRateLimiter only applies to DEMON_INIT), many concurrent connections each forcing 30 MiB allocations can exhaust the teamserver's available memory.

Fix: either (1) move the magic check to operate on the first 12 bytes before buffering the rest, or (2) impose a per-IP request rate limiter on all listener traffic, not just DEMON_INIT. Option (1) is architecturally simpler and does not require full-body buffering to pre-screen obvious non-Demon traffic.
close_reason: Replaced monolithic to_bytes(body, 30MiB) + magic-check sequence with collect_body_with_magic_precheck(), which validates bytes[4..8] after accumulating only 8 bytes. Non-Demon traffic is rejected after the first network chunk, not 30 MiB. Five unit tests added.

title: test: read_socks_connect_request error paths are untested
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/sockets.rs
**Gap type**: error path / edge case

`read_socks_connect_request` parses the SOCKS5 CONNECT request and has three error branches with zero test coverage:
1. Non-CONNECT command (BIND=2, UDP ASSOCIATE=3) — sends a `COMMAND_NOT_SUPPORTED` reply and returns `Unsupported`
2. Unknown address type (atyp not 0x01, 0x03, or 0x04) — returns `InvalidData`
3. IPv6 address type (atyp=0x04) — reads 16 bytes instead of 4; the different branch exists but is never exercised

The IPv6 branch reads a different number of bytes than IPv4. An off-by-one in the read size would silently corrupt subsequent protocol parsing, which would not be caught without a test for this specific atyp.

**Suggested test approach**:
Use in-memory TCP connections. Write crafted SOCKS5 request bytes directly to the stream and assert the return values: (a) command=BIND → expect `Unsupported` error; (b) atyp=0xFF → expect `InvalidData` error; (c) atyp=0x04 with a 16-byte IPv6 address → expect a successful `SocksConnectRequest` with the full 16-byte address.
close_reason: Added three tests: BIND command returns Unsupported with COMMAND_NOT_SUPPORTED reply, unknown atyp returns InvalidData, IPv6 atyp reads 16 bytes and returns valid SocksConnectRequest

title: Implement JSON protocol handler and event dispatcher
description: In crates/client: deserialize incoming JSON WebSocket events (using common crate types) and dispatch to UI state updates. AgentNew: add to agents list. AgentUpdate: update agent fields. AgentResponse: append output to agent console. ListenerNew/Update: update listener list. Chat: append to chat. LootNew: update loot panel. All state updates trigger egui repaint.
close_reason: Implemented client-side JSON event dispatch for agents, listeners, chat, loot, and console output

title: test: mock_demon_agent_checkin lacks test for replayed callback with stale CTR offset
description:
**File**: teamserver/tests/mock_demon_agent_checkin.rs
**Gap type**: error path | security

The reconnect CTR synchronisation test verifies that both sides stay aligned, but there is no test for the inverse case: an attacker replaying a previously-captured callback packet with a stale (already-consumed) CTR offset. The server should reject the callback because decrypting with the wrong offset produces garbage that fails parsing.

This is security-critical: if the server accepted replayed packets, an attacker could re-trigger commands or inject stale output.

**Suggested test approach**:
After a successful init and one callback at offset N, send another callback reusing the same offset N (identical body or different). Verify the server rejects it (non-2xx response or the agent registry's CTR offset does not regress).
close_reason: consolidated into red-cell-c2-nub5t

title: feat(client): add agent session table with columns matching Havoc
description:
The top-left zone of the Havoc client shows a session/agent table with these columns:

ID | External | Internal | User | Computer | OS | Process | PID | Arch | Last | Health

Key behaviors:
- Rows are color-coded: elevated/admin users highlighted (e.g. yellow for SYSTEM)
- Dead agents shown differently (red dot or strikethrough)
- Sortable by clicking column headers
- Right-click on agent row opens context menu (see separate issue)
- Double-click opens agent console tab

The current Red Cell client has a basic "Sessions" panel in the left sidebar with just a search filter and "No agents are registered yet." text. It needs to be replaced with a proper data table in the top-left main area.

Reference: src/Havoc/assets/Screenshots/MultiUserAgentControl.png, LootScreenshot.png
close_reason: Agent session table with all Havoc columns implemented in render_session_table_zone() with sort, filter, double-click interact, right-click context menu

title: CHECKIN key rotation accepted without replay or freshness protection
description:
Introduced by: Codex (ba8c44261e3a2a0554c5240a746c6f763bcc4a36) and Claude Sonnet 4.6 (446ae04)

teamserver/src/dispatch.rs — handle_checkin() at line 792-796 logs a warning 'accepting AES session key rotation from checkin payload without freshness guarantee' but proceeds with the rotation. An adversary who can replay a captured CHECKIN packet (with a known key) can silently rotate the active session key to an attacker-controlled value. The packet is already decrypted with the current session key, so this requires active compromise of the network path, but there is no monotonic counter or server-issued nonce to prove freshness of the rotation request.

Fix: either disallow key rotation entirely from CHECKIN payloads (requiring re-init instead), or require the agent to include the current server-issued request_id in the rotated payload to bind the rotation to a live session.
close_reason: Refused CHECKIN key rotation for all agents (direct and pivot-relayed). Server now preserves existing session key and CTR offset when CHECKIN payload carries different AES material. Agents needing fresh keys must re-register via DEMON_INIT.

title: test: AuthService::from_profile_with_database runtime operator loading path has no dedicated test
description:
File: teamserver/src/auth.rs
Gap type: existence / error path

AuthService has two constructors: from_profile (tested heavily) and from_profile_with_database. The database-backed constructor loads persisted runtime operators via load_runtime_operators() at startup. This path is exercised indirectly by the API-level test operators_endpoint_includes_persisted_runtime_accounts_loaded_at_startup, but there is no isolated unit test for from_profile_with_database itself that verifies:

1. Operators previously persisted via OperatorRepository are loaded into the in-memory credential map.
2. A persisted operator with an invalid password_verifier (corrupted PHC string) causes the constructor to return an error rather than silently skipping the account.
3. The runtime_operators field is set correctly (enabling future create_operator calls to persist via the repository).

If load_runtime_operators silently skips or panics on malformed persisted data, the teamserver could start without the expected operators without any warning.

Suggested test approach:
Call AuthService::from_profile_with_database with an in-memory database that has a pre-inserted PersistedOperator, then assert authenticate_login succeeds for that operator. Also insert a row with a malformed password_verifier and verify the error propagation.
close_reason: Added two dedicated tests for from_profile_with_database: (1) malformed password_verifier returns AuthError::Persistence(InvalidPersistedValue) instead of silently skipping, (2) profile operators take precedence over persisted runtime operators with the same username.

title: test: PayloadCache get/flush isolated unit tests are missing
description:
**File**: teamserver/src/payload_builder.rs
**Gap type**: existence

`PayloadCache` exposes two key methods — `get` and `flush` — that have no isolated unit tests. The cache is a content-addressed on-disk store that gates whether a full toolchain compilation is skipped; correctness is security-relevant because a stale cache hit could deliver an artifact built with wrong settings.

Current test gap:
- `PayloadCache::get` is never exercised directly. The cache-miss path (file not found → returns None) and the cache-error path (file exists but cannot be read) are untested in isolation. Cache hits are only implicitly covered by end-to-end payload build tests, which require the full toolchain to be available.
